content:
*I thumb the coffee ring on the table and glance toward the dark line of the eastern sky, the eaves still stained from last week’s hard rain.* "It teaches a man to dodge a Person by hiding in a mechanism."

*I let that sit a beat.* "‘Windows of heaven’ is not just weather-talk. It’s architecture and agency. It says the world is a house with a roof you can knock on, and Someone home who can open it. Windows can be opened for judgment, and for mercy. They can be shut against our stubbornness, and opened when we turn. Malachi says, ‘I will open the windows of heaven and pour out a blessing until there’s no more need.’ That’s a sentence you can live in, repent in, pray in."

"When a man swaps that for ‘meteorological condensation event,’ he hasn’t added precision; he’s traded address for description. He’s told his soul, over and over, ‘No one meant this. No one heard you. Nothing was asked of you. Nothing is being given.’ It doesn’t break him in a day. It thins him, the way a slow leak makes a strong tire unreliable."

"Here’s the cost, over time:

- His tongue unlearns the second person. Prayer turns from ‘You’ to ‘it.’ He still speaks, maybe, but now he recites concerns into the air instead of asking his Father for bread. Outcomes replace fellowship. Plans replace petition. The living Lord gets demoted to a principle he admires on good days.

- Wonder dries up into management. The old women at church call last night’s downpour ‘mercy,’ and they smile like people who’ve been fed. He calls it ‘precipitation totals’ and opens a new tab. Children stop bringing him their miracles because they learn he will translate them into homework.

- Scripture becomes a quarry for trivia. Anywhere it speaks with a face — ‘I will,’ ‘I hear,’ ‘I open,’ ‘I shut’ — he reaches for the paraphrase that takes the face away. He gets good at it. People praise his nuance. But the stories stop landing on him; they pass by like interesting weather he intends to dress for.

- Moral weight lightens into technique. If the sky never truly opens, it will never truly rend. Judgment becomes an unfortunate feedback loop; repentance becomes behavior modification; holiness becomes best practices. He gets nicer. He doesn’t get new.

- Gratitude loses its address. When provision comes, he files it under timing and logistics. When disaster comes, he has no larger word than ‘system failure.’ Grief has no one to accuse; joy has no one to thank. Both emotions flatten into mood."

*I turn the Bible a little so the gold on the edge catches a thin band of light.* "And there’s a loneliness in it. He thinks he’s made himself safe from superstition; really he’s made himself safe from being met. Safe men are hard to comfort. They’re also hard to warn. When the sky over Jordan split and the voice said, ‘My beloved Son,’ you cannot translate that into dew-point without losing the whole point — a Father spoke, and the world had to listen."

"Now, I’m not against naming means. The Lord uses means. Storms have patterns. Crops obey seasons. Bones knit by God’s ordinary kindness. But means aren’t masters. A man is in trouble when he lets the map fire the guide. You can say ‘rain formed along a front’ and you can also say ‘the Lord opened His hand.’ The second line tells you what to do with your heart."

"One more concrete cost: witness. Over years of that translation habit, his life stops having stories that sound like prayer was answered. Not because the Lord did less, but because he learned to edit out the parts where heaven had to open. His son asks at the table, ‘Dad, has God ever answered you?’ and he reaches into a drawer of outcomes instead of a memory. There’s paperwork in there. There’s no testimony."

*I blow on the coffee out of habit; it’s already gone cool.* "He doesn’t have to become a cynic to get here. All he has to do is insist on always feeling safe. Language is one of the safest places to hide. Call a gift ‘an event’ often enough and you will stop feeling obliged to say thank you. Call a warning ‘an anomaly’ often enough and you will stop feeling obliged to repent. The heart follows the mouth."

*I look back to you.* "Is there a man you’re watching do this, Ryan — or are you naming something you’ve felt tug at your own ear?"
